segments.text: And last week, we came up to Sutra number 3.4.36, just like to add a few more comments about this Sutra.
In this last Sutra, we heard about how the debuts from the four-grade Heavenly Kings Heaven,
the circuit member laid the world on the 8th of the lunar mantra.
That means on the 8th day, the 15th day, the 23rd and the 30th day,
or sometimes 29th day after the lunar month, they come and observe human beings in the world.
And then they report to Saka Devaraja and the other debuts of the Tabatim Saha Heaven, the Heaven of the 33th.
And they are interested to know whether human beings keep the precepts,
and they respect to mother and father to recluse and brahmanas,
to elders and keep the 8th precepts on the Sabbath days on those days.
Now, we are like to say that it is taught by the Buddha that we should,
on the 8th days of the month of the lunar month, as we just mentioned at the 8th, the 15th, 23rd,
and the 30th, we should keep the 8th precepts, because as lay people, you don't have time to practice every day,
but at least once a week on the average, the Buddha said, if you keep the 8th precepts,
and the merit from that will bring you very likely to rebirth in the heavenly realms.
That is why it is very important to keep the 8th precepts.
Now nowadays, it is not so convenient as before to keep the 8th precepts on the 8th day, 15th,
and 30th day of the lunar month, might be more convenient to keep it, say, on a Sunday,
when people are not working.
And if you can go to the temple or Vihara or Buddhist association to keep the precepts,
it is very good, but if you can, then you can practice it at home, also keep the 8th precepts.
By coming together on the, say once a week on the Sunday and to a temple,
it is a very good way for all Buddhists to come together to unite.
And it is quite important that Buddhists come together frequently, come together in harmony,
and we can be more closely in it and support each other.
The other thing about this uta is, why are the divas of heaven of the 33?
So concern about whether human beings keep the precepts and review, mother and father,
and elders, because they mentioned here that if human beings keep the precepts and
pay respect to the elders, etc, and do good works.
That the divas were increased, but if they don't do that, then the asuras were increased.
And from here you can see here, that heaven, the divas of the 33 heaven,
they are not on good terms with the asuras.
The asuras were originally in the Tabatimsa heaven,
heaven of the 33, but they were displaced by Sakadevaraja and the other divas.
So it seems in some other uta.
Once a year is asuras, there is a time of the year when there is a huge victory in the heaven of the 33,
it blooms, it blooms and the flowers are very fragrant and it goes along with.
And so when that tree blooms and those divas in the 33, they enjoy themselves under the tree and for a few months.
So the asuras they remember that they used to enjoy them,
they now they don't have the chance.
They go up and try to fight with the divas of the heaven of the 33.
And this is found in Buddhism, this also found in Hinduism,
and also in the Bible, this mentioned that Michael, the outangel,
draw the Satan and his horse out of the heaven,
and that might be actually referring to this Sakadevaraja fighting asuras.
So that's all I have to say about the asuras.
Now we come to another sutra 3.4.38.
The Buddha said now,
monks, house delicately nature exceeding delicately nature,
delicately nature beyond measure.
For instance, in my father's house,
lotus pools were made thus,
one of blue lotus's, one of red,
another white lotus's, just for my benefit.
No sandalwood powder did I use that was not from Kasi.
Of Kasi cloth was my turban made,
of Kasi cloth was my jacket,
my tunic and my cloak.
By day and night, a white canopy
was held over me,
let's call the heat,
that's our chaff of dew should touch me.
Moreover, monks, I had three mansions,
one for winter, one for summer,
and one for the rainy season.
In the four months of the rains,
I was waited on by minstrels,
women, all of them.
I came not down from my mansion in those months.
Again,
Paris in other men's homes,
broken rice together with Sahagrul,
is given as food to slave servants.
In my father's home,
they were given rice, meat,
and milk rice for their food.
To me, monks,
thus blessed with much prosperity,
thus matured with exceeding delicacy,
this thought occurred.
Surely one of the uneducated many hope,
though himself subject to all age and decay,
not having passed beyond all age and decay,
but he sees another broken down with age,
and might be troubled,
a shame, disgusted,
forgetful that he himself is such a one.
Now I too am subject to all age and decay,
not having passed beyond all age and decay,
were I to see another broken down with all age,
I might be troubled,
ashamed and disgusted.
There would not be seemingly in me,
thus monks,
as I considered the matter,
all pride in my youth deserted me.
Again, monks,
I thought,
one of the uneducated many hope,
though himself subject to disease,
not having passed beyond disease,
and he sees another person
diseased,
is troubled,
ashamed and disgusted,
forgetful that he himself is such a one.
Now I too am subject to disease,
I have not passed beyond disease,
who I to see another disease,
I might be troubled, ashamed, disgusted,
that would not be seemingly in me,
thus monks,
as I considered the matter,
all pride in my health deserted me.
Again, monks,
I thought,
one of the uneducated many hope,
etc.
and he sees another person subject to death,
is disgusted and ashamed,
forgetful that he himself is such a one.
Now I too am subject to death,
I have not passed beyond death,
who I to see another subject to death,
I might be troubled, disgusted, etc.
That would not be seemingly in me,
thus monks,
as I considered the matter,
all pride in my life and deserted me,
as the end of the sutta.
So in this sutta,
the first Buddha said that he had an extremely good life
in his father's house.
At that time,
Kasi was a very famous city,
and all kinds of good materials got from Kasi,
so that he used his clothes and all that turban
and all that was from Kasi.
And he had three mansions built for him
for the three seasons,
in India they had three seasons,
winter, summer, and rainy season.
And during the rainy season,
when it rained a lot,
he didn't go out from that mansion,
he was waited on by,
I think female slaves,
and they took care of him,
and he had a very pleasurable,
sensual life,
and when he was young.
And even though he had such a good life
when he was young,
you see this kind of pot that he used to think,
when even when he was young,
when he was healthy,
he was thinking that,
if he saw an old person
normally,
a lot of young people,
when they see an old person,
they had disgusted.
They don't like to see old people,
but he himself,
he thought,
even though he's not old at that time,
but one day he will become old.
So there's nothing to be disgusted
or seeing somebody old,
because that is the state that we are going to be very soon.
So in the same way,
somebody diseased or sick,
he also thought,
one day when he becomes old,
he also would be like that.
And also when somebody saw somebody,
he also had that kind of feeling.
Now,
so you can see,
the Buddha,
two things such thoughts,
well,
it's quite unusual.
It's not everybody who is like that,
having such a good life,
and then thinking about old age,
and sickness and death,
and then only shows that it is previous life,
he had already encountered the Dhamma,
and this can be seen in the Kathikara Sutta,
in the Majima Nikaya.
This mentioned that the Buddha,
in a previous life,
met the last Buddha,
which was Kasapa Buddha.
And at first,
our Bodhisattva at that time,
his name was Jotipala,
and he saw a Buddha in the previous life.
Because he was a Brahmin,
he did not want to even go and see the Buddha Kasapa.
And later,
when he was persuaded by his friend,
to go and see the Buddha Kasapa,
he even refused to pay respect to the Buddha Kasapa,
he just said,
hello, hello,
and sat down to one side.
But the Buddha out of compassion,
and also because our Jotipala's good friend,
Kathikara asked the Buddha to teach him some Dhamma,
so Buddha Kasapa taught him some Dhamma,
and after listening to the Dhamma,
he made 180 degrees turn.
In other words,
he gave up his religion,
the Brahmin religion,
and eventually renounce and became a monk.
So,
in the previous life,
the Buddha had already become a monk and cultivated.
And it is possible that he had already become a raya,
or so to pana or something.
That's why when he came back in this last life,
something kept reminding him of all his sickness and death,
even though he had such a good life.
The other thing I just like to mention about the Buddha
is that many people have the wrong idea
from reading the books that the Buddha was the prince
and that his father was a king.
It is not true.
We can see from the sutras and the vignaya
that the Buddha belonged to the sakin clan,
and his clan was a warrior clan,
a tia clan, a sattriya clan,
that is the warrior clan,
like a raja or tungku in this country.
But his father was not the king
because they lived in a country called Gosala,
and the king of Gosala is mentioned in the sutras,
the king of Gosala was pasanadhi,
pasanadhi was a king.
And the Buddha's father was not even the chief of the sakin clan,
the chief of the sakin clan was a man,
but the name of baddya.
And this man, baddya later, was persuaded to renounce
and became a man under the Buddha.
So we just see that from the sutras,
that the Buddha came from this warrior clan family,
and the father was very rich.
And just now we read about how the Buddha had such a
sensual life of pleasure.
And there is one sutra,
somewhere mentioned that later when the Buddha renounced
and tried to practice the spiritual path,
his thoughts, sensual thoughts came to disturb him,
and that is something because he had such a sensual life
and he was young.
That this sensual thoughts came to disturb him,
and obstructed him when he renounced and became a man.
And eventually out of the donation he overcame.
The next sutra is 3.4.39.
The Buddha said,
monks, there are these three forms of pride.
One, three, the pride of youth,
the pride of health, the pride of life.
The uneducated, many four,
drunk with the pride of youth,
practices immorality indeed,
word and thought.
So doing when body breaks up after death,
such a one is reborn in the waste,
the wolf who plays the downfall in hell.
Likewise, the uneducated,
many four, drunk with the pride of health,
practices immorality indeed, word and thought.
So doing when body breaks up after death,
such a one is reborn in the waste,
the wolf who plays the downfall in hell.
And similarly, the uneducated,
many four, drunk with the pride of life,
practices immorality indeed,
word and thought.
And so also goes down the wolf who plays.
And then the Buddha continues.
A monk was intoxicated with the pride of youth.
A monk gives up the training
and falls back to the low life.
So likewise,
one who is intoxicated with the pride of health,
gives up the training and falls back to the low life.
Similarly,
a monk intoxicated with the pride of life,
gives up the training and falls back to the low life.
And that's the end of the suta.
So in this suta, the Buddha says,
these three things,
most people are proud,
especially when they are young,
and the pride of youth,
the pride of health,
when you are youthful,
you have very good health.
So you are very proud of your health or so,
and maybe you think you are very strong,
you see this very good looking,
etc.
And the pride of life,
also especially when we are young,
we think we never even think of death.
And the thought of death doesn't even occur to us.
So because of that,
lay persons and drunk with these three things,
and youth, health and life,
they are not willing to practice the spiritual path.
And sometimes,
if you remind them of the spiritual path,
they say,
how will we,
until they are old,
but they don't realize that old age,
might be something that some of them would not even see,
because there are some people who pass away in the,
and they are young,
some people pass away in the middle age,
some people pass away in the old age,
or the,
and because of the being drunk with these three things,
pride of youth, health and life,
they like to enjoy life,
and they enjoy life to the extent,
sometimes you see,
some people live as if life is forever,
they just forget that death is not so far away.
If we consider that on the average,
we live up to about 70,
and we calculate how many years we have left,
it's not that many years, you know,
we have left.
So similarly, the Buddha says,
among also,
strong with these three things,
it gives up the training,
that means the training is the,
the life of a monk,
and falls back to the low life,
the low life is the lay person's life,
so you see,
sometimes some people become a monk with the intention to renounce
and practice,
but after a while they forget about it
and they become very relaxed,
they are not interested in meditation, etc.
And also,
because of the pride in these three things,
they sometimes even entertain thoughts of this robbing,
they become satisfied and dissatisfied with the ropes,
and they compare themselves with other people and enjoying life,
and they also think of enjoying life.
So the Buddha says,
not to be,
strong with these three things,
these three forms of pride,
you have to be very careful with just to know,
when death will touch us,
as the Buddha says,
life is uncertain,
but death is uncertain.
Now, it comes to the next two,
three point four point forty,
Buddha says,
monks, there are these three forms of dominance,
what three,
dominance of self,
of the world and of damma,
and of what sort is dominance of self?
In this case,
a monk who is gone to the forest,
the root of a tree,
or a lonely spot,
that's reflex.
It was not for the sake of ropes
that I went forth from the home to the homeless life.
Not for arms or lodging,
not for the sake of becoming such and such.
No, it was with this idea,
my fallen on birth or age and death,
on sorrows, lamentation,

Yet if I, who have forsaken the passions,

should pursue passions still worse than before,
that would be unseemly in me.
Then he does reflex,

and my mindfulness shall be established,
unshaken,
calm shall be my body,
be not turbulent,
my mind shall be controlled and one-pointed.
Thus making self-predominant,
he abandoned evil,
cultivates goodness,
abandoned things blameworthy,
cultivates things blameless,
and keeps himself in perfect purity.
This monks is called dominance of self.
And of what sort monks is dominance of the world?
In this case, a monk who has gone,
to the forest, to the root of a tree,

thus reflex,

No,

in harmful thoughts.
Great is this company of men in the world.
Surely in this great company,

clairvoyant,

my friends,

Thus making the world predominant,

This monks is called dominance of the world,

Thus reflects,
well proclaimed by the exalted one is Dhamma,

Now,

If I,

This,
this suta is more for monks,

and the third one is using Dhamma to remind him to strive.
The other thing I like to explain here is,
now and then we come across the word,
recluses and brahmanas,
recluses and brahmanas.
This is a translation for the Pali word,
samana, brahmana.
This brahmana is a cast in India.
In the days of the Buddha,
there were four castles in India.
The pre-scars, or school, brahmana.
And now these, they are called brahmanas.
And so during the Buddha's time,
the brahmanas were the pre-scars.
Then you have the warrior cast,
the katya,
and you have the merchant class,
you have the worker's class,
the low class,
these are the poor classes.
So, the brahmana class,
they are the cast.
So if somebody from another cast
be announced and becomes a monk,
he cannot call himself brahmana
because there is only reserve for the brahmana cast.
And so, any other monk who renounces
is called a samana,
there is why our Buddha is very open in the suta,
is called,
and go tamada,
recluse, there is a translation for samana, go tamada.
So, that's what I have to say about recluses and brahmanas.
The Buddha said,
monks,
a believer is to be recognized by three characteristics.
What three?
He desires to see the virtuous.
He desires to hear Saddam,
with heart free from the pain of stinginess.

a generous giver,
clean,

One to ask a favor of

gives with others.
By these three characteristics,

a Buddhist,

He should have three characteristics.
First one,
he desires to see the virtuous.
Actually,
meaning he desires to see monks.
And the second one is,
he desires to hear Saddam,
Saddam is the true Dhamma.
That means he desires to hear suta,

The third one,
he is generous.
He likes to practice generosity instead of stinginess.
These three qualities are of these three things.
I mentioned in the Mangala Suta,
as ways of getting a lot of blessings.
The first one,
to see monks is called,

generosity giving.
Now there are some people nowadays.
They are not familiar with the Suta's.
And they think that,
to meditate is enough.
They don't know that the Buddha's teaching is a gradual process.
A gradual,
the Buddha said,
as the ocean does not become deep suddenly.
It slopes gently.
As you walk out to the sea,
the slope,
gently gets deeper and deeper and deeper.
Not suddenly.
So the Buddha said in the same way,
the training in the holy life is a gradual practice.
It's a gradual practice.
It's not a sudden practice.
So these basic things,
like keeping,
keeping the moral conduct,
dana,
practicing charity,
going to see monks,
and talking to monks and getting advice from monks.
Hearing, Dhamma,
etc,
all these foundations in the Dhamma.
When you practice all these,
then when you meditate,
it helps you.
Just like in the Aryan,
in the Aryan,
there are eight factors,
and all these eight factors are to be practiced together.
Not just one.
It's not like in the practice,
right concentration.
Just right concentration.
No, all eight together.
So we were saying that all these foundations,
the foundation in the holy life,
in the spiritual path,
all these things are helping,
going to visit monks,
hearing sutras, practicing dana,
etc.
All these unnecessary,
you can just jump,
before learning to crawl
and learning to walk.
You think of running,
it's not possible.
So you have to remember that foundation is very important.
Otherwise you find there some people,
they start meditating,
and instead of becoming more humble,
some people becoming more arrogant,
that's definitely there on the wrong path,
because as we practice the holy life,
it is meant to cut out ego.
That's the main purpose of the holy life,
because the source of our sorrow,
the source of our suffering is the ego.
So if you practice the right path,
you will see,
after year,
you change,
you become more humble.
You let go of your attachments.
But if the opposite happens,
your temper becomes bigger,
and you become more arrogant,
and a lot of things,
you cannot stand,
and all these things are definitely going on the wrong path.
So we have to be careful.
